posts.name: Brian Buzzini
Randy Ch@ppel
posts.cooked:
I bought those 2 and still have them. Also have a good amount of 72-80 Chappellet — love those wines.

More interesting maybe is that I have two bottles with this identical label but no vintage stated. Winery told me those are most likely the 1968 vintage. They told me they made wines in 67 and 68 from estate grown grapes with the wines cellared at Mondavi and bottled at Schramsberg. The on-site winery wasn’t finished until late 1968 and that made 1969 their first official vintage.

posts.name: Justin Goodman
posts.cooked: First bottle(s) I’ve seen come to market in a while. I collect old Napa and have been hunting this wine for a while, but not a buyer at $10k.

posts.name: Rich Brown
posts.cooked:
HoosJustinG:

First bottle(s) I’ve seen come to market in a while. I collect old Napa and have been hunting this wine for a while, but not a buyer at $10k.

Yep, i feel the same. My cellar is pretty heavy with aged CA and id love to snag a bottle… but I’m not interested at all at $10k.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
AndyK:

Can someone explain what’s so special about the 69?

Look at CT ratings of this wine over the past few years: 100, 98, 98, 100, 98, 97, 100, 99.

I have a friend who is a Napa nut with more than 10k bottles of Napa in his cellar, a house in Napa (but he‘s from Europe) and a side business of importing Napa wines and he considers this the best Napa he ever had.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked:
Randy,

Congrats on the profitable purchase. I have had a lot of great old Napa wines from the '70’s, Diamond Creek, Mondavi Reserve, Stag’s Leap, Montelena, Phelps and I really love these wines, but I find it hard to place a 4 digit price tag on any of them. I have not had the '69, but I see some posters noting this bottle is considered the best ever Napa by some so I guess that is the reason for bidding wars.

posts.name: Karl K@zaks
posts.cooked: About seven years ago, I got case of the 74 Sterling Rev CS. I remember one bottle so fresh I wondered if it had gotten more sulfur than the other bottles.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
Maybe back in 1999 or 2000 I got a couple magnums of Chappellet Cab, I think vintages might have been 77 and 78, and they were only ‘ok’ not as radiant as I’d hoped. This must have been around the time that Parker had mentioned the 69 being a stunner, but it was a throwaway comment in his normal reviews of the 97 (I think) so the world had yet to cotton on to those older ones. Those mags were pretty cheap even in the context of that era, hardly much more than what prevailing releases were (on a 750ml basis).

For anyone motivated, Doug Shafer had a good interview with one of the Chappellet family on a podcast, which I listened to in the last week. It’s interesting for those who want to learn more about that Togni (and later) era. I’ve “read” some of Molly Chappellet picture/coffee table books too, which are neat, but not worth the money.

posts.name: Eric Ifune
posts.cooked: The 1969 Chappellet was an instant legend when it was released. Everyone, including the Brits, said it was Chateau Latour in California and it made growers search out mountain vineyards. Generally on the short list of greatest California wines.

posts.name: Randy Ch@ppel
posts.cooked:
um . . . am I reading it right? One went for $16,000 and the other for $17,700?

I guess if anyone wants to trade me say, a 6 pack owc of 98 Petrus for one of my bottles, please let me know

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: I know there are some questions about the price here, but in the context of expensive hobbies, the buyer has got the most desirable and rare old California cabernet for the cost of a good minor painting.

posts.name: Rich K0rz€nk0
posts.cooked: I try not to judge, it has a uniqueness to it; just seems like that painting was bought on a cruise ship.

posts.name: Justin Goodman
posts.cooked:
It’s a lot of money … but also … if you’re shopping for a single bottle of wine and have the budget to spend $17k on it … you can buy a random bottle of DRC, Leroy, Leflaive Monty, etc from any random vintage — something that, if you drink in that circle, you’re probably going to see again, and know several people that own lots of them.

Or … you could buy a legendary bottle (that is, by all accounts, a fantastic wine) from one of the world’s most important wine regions that you’ll probably never see again. I know personally that if I walked into a tasting and saw the table lined up with GC Burgundy, First Growths, Screagle, etc … and the 69 Chappellet, I’d 100% be most excited to taste the 69 Chappellet.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked: Are those older Chappellet’s bottled up with fruit that now goes into the ‘Pritchard Hill’ line?

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked: But a bottle of wine has zero in common with a painting as a collectible. I guess you could frame it and hang it on the wall with a spotlight in your wine cellar. It’s still pretty much gonna look like just a bottle of wine though.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Marcu_Stanley:

But a bottle of wine has zero in common with a painting as a collectible. I guess you could frame it and hang it on the wall with a spotlight in your wine cellar. It’s still pretty much gonna look like just a bottle of wine though.

Not quite. There are a few things that they have in common, they take up time and the resources of the collector. They also provide pleasure, fulfill passion and to some degree, add status. The big difference of course is one is a consumable, one is not. I kind of like the idea of wine, where the pleasure is not just in acquisition, but opening a bottle of something utterly unique and perfect with friends.

posts.name: Andy K0b3rl
posts.cooked:
Mark_Golodetz:

I kind of like the idea of wine, where the pleasure is not just in acquisition, but opening a bottle of something utterly unique and perfect with friends.

People can also get pleasure from viewing the artwork (not just acquiring it), provided it’s not stored in a warehouse, of course

posts.name: Justin Goodman
posts.cooked:
Marcu_Stanley:

But a bottle of wine has zero in common with a painting as a collectible. I guess you could frame it and hang it on the wall with a spotlight in your wine cellar. It’s still pretty much gonna look like just a bottle of wine though.

Plenty of collectibles have nearly zero utility other than as a collectible … stamps, ancient coins, trading cards, etc. Even steel watches have almost no intrinsic value … a $50 Timex keeps better time than a $250k Patek … but only one is a collectible.

posts.name: ken emery
posts.cooked: I’ve actually had this wine multiple times as a member of a tasting group I was in had a case of it and he was into sharing. IIRC this wine was made by Phillip Togni. The two times I can remember having the 1969 Chappelet it was spectacular, one of the best Napa Cabs I’d ever had if not the best.

posts.name: Richard Levin
posts.cooked: A new bottle has appeared on Winebid for over $16k. Guess as a result of this auction in April. Also noticed a magnum from Phelps cellar in this week’s HDH auction with an estimated high price of $5k. That’s still pretty steep. I had this wine around 15 years ago and thought it was pretty special but needed more time. I like California Cabernet from this era and it’s amazing how many of them have hung in there after all this time. Particularly, Heitz and Robert Mondavi.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: It’s the best California Cabernet I ever tasted, not much question about that.

posts.name: M a r k K a p l a n
posts.cooked:
I guess it’s worth excavating my dog’s breakfast of a basement home cellar to see if I have any remaining. I think there may be one.

1968 received all the praise but, in my experience, 1969 also produced some great wines. My favorite was Mondavi Unfiltered, better than Reseve by a margin. Although it’s been a minute since I’ve tasted any from the vintage.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: He has had a remarkable career! For me, 1992 is my favorite mature vintage from his own property. 2001 has been a very slow burn but is beginning to open up. 2013 is also pretty monumental. Of course, his daughter Lisa has been involved for quite a while now and today has the reins. Togni’s wines are the biggest Napa holding in my cellar today.

posts.name: Randy Ch@ppel
posts.cooked: folks also famously raved about his final vintage at Cuvaison in 1975 where his “signature” bottle got rave reviews. I’ve had a couple of those in my cellar over the years (one remains) and it has been a great wine. Last week one popped up on WineBid – very rare and unusual – and sold for something like $800 all-in.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Have tasted the creditable '75 Zinfandel but never the signature…

posts.name: Randy Ch@ppel
posts.cooked: happy to open it together if you’re ever in Dallas!

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked:
As the happy owner of a half a case of the 1992 and over a case of the 2001 this answer makes me feel very smug! Pretty much all of my Cali holdings are Togni, Ridge, and Dominus in that order. Togni in particular was radically underpriced for the quality for years, and to some degree still is.

I’ve found the bottle to bottle quality of both vintages to vary a bit (possibly because I’ve sourced mine from the secondary market)…the 1992 at its best can be absolutely mind-bending, one of those wines that transforms repeatedly in the glass. I’ve had 2001s that span the range from seemingly not yet ready/a bit shut down to almost on the downslope, but when they hit they are fantastic

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: The late Max Marinucci introduced me to Togni, bringing a 1993 to a lunch. I had it recently, it still shows well, but for me the 1997 has always been the pick of the bunch. A couple of years ago, I served it out of magnum with some fine Bordeaux including some first growths. It came second behind a Latour 1990, and beating some pretty serious wines.
